# Transcrição: 02- Course 6 (Lesson 1) - The Four Quadrants Of Real Estate

- **Fonte:** 02- Course 6 (Lesson 1) - The Four Quadrants Of Real Estate
- **Duração:** 05:24
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

The four quadrants are real estate investing that I use. These four quadrants can be used in almost any business transaction and should be. I remember when I first got out of college I was selling automobiles because I couldn't get a job doing anything else. When you're buying a car, typically the buyer, the customer would come to me and they were always focused on one thing. In most cases that was the price. While it might make sense, it's not the way to negotiate when you're buying real estate. The reality is when you're buying a car there is more involved than just the price. There's the price from the very assertive buyer. How much? How much? How much? Don't be that guy when you're buying real estate by the way. Then there's the down payment. How much equity will you need to get your deal financed in the case of a car deal? How much money do you have to put down? Which leads us to the monthly payment and in most cases they're trading something in. Those are the four quadrants of a car deal. When you're buying real estate, don't lock on one quadrant. Work them all. In this industry, the four quadrants in real estate investing are price, equity or down payment, the NOI and the cash on cash. Now I could expand these four quadrants out but for right now, and I will by the way, for right now I want you to focus on the four. Keep in mind as one quadrant moves so do the others. A shift in price for example will increase the down payment or equity needed either up or down. And that will not change your NOI but it will affect your cash on cash. The NOI is the NOI. So you have a price in one quadrant, down payment in another, NOI which gives you your cash on cash. Every quadrant is influenced for the most part by the movement of the others. And all quadrants matter. Don't get locked on one. There's no such thing as a free lunch or a zero down. For every course that I've seen on NO Money Down, I knew we were either talking about calling sellers, cold calling sellers, negotiating with sellers for a contract, flipping deals. This is typically not real estate investing when you hear about NO Money Down. When I talk about these four quadrants, someone in Everly pushes back with a zero down scenario. So theoretically, if you're not putting money down, would you have zero in a quadrant? I wouldn't. Either investors will be involved, that's their money down. Or I'm going to be promising something to the seller to hold money in the future and that would go in that quadrant. Without digging too deep, I'll just say this. There's really no such thing as NO Money Down.

## [03:01 - 05:24]

Someone is promising someone something in the future. In every real estate transaction, there is an obligation to something so something would go in that quadrant. So if you secure a piece of property with no commitment to money down, you'd have to question just how good is that property. The mistakes real estate investors make too often is they complicate and or act like they're getting something for nothing. It's impossible. Again, the best deals are going to require a price, a down payment, debt, and hopefully it's going to provide you with cash flow. Too often, even though the quadrants make your process simple and clear cut, new investors still make the same few mistakes over and over. Number one, they don't know their market. Know your market. Two, you get fixated on one of these quadrants. Oh, I'm going to buy at the lowest price or I'm going to get the highest cash on cash or I did it with no money down. Don't get focused or fixated on a quadrant. By the way, most new investors miss on the NOI, which is probably the most important of the four quadrants. They try to buy something with no money down because they don't have any money down rather than going to uncles, aunts, moms, and dads and raising the necessary funds to get money down. So again, price is in your top quadrant. Money down is going to be required at some point, either now or in the future, even if you're promising a seller-owned financing. Three, your NOI. You need to know what the worst case scenario NOI is. You want to do your pencil, gross revenue, less expenses, cut in half. Boom, 50% NOI. Know that your expenses should be 35 to 44. That would be your higher NOI. Look at your trailing 12 for another quadrant and say, this could be my worst case NOI and that leaves me with my cash on cash. And I've said it enough times. Hopefully, cash on cash is the holy grail. I will have a cash on cash if I have interest only and I should also have a cash on cash even with an interest only loan for principle and interest, which will sooner or later be an obligation to you as the buyer. Work out your four quadrants.
